data: A tall base of pink veined breccia, finished on all four sides with moulding top and bottom. The upper surface has a circular hole to the rear left (approx. 6cm diameter) and a foot hole (approx 25 x 5 cm) front right. There is light damage to the lower moulding on the rear corners and to the upper moulding on all sides. The base is D 85 cm x W 84 cm; the top is D 71.7 cm x W 64 cm (damaged). The pillar (H 84.5 cm) narrows slightly towards the top (D 51.5 cm to 48.2 cm; W 52 cm to 49 cm). Total height is 125.5 cm.
Three lines of Latin letters, centred on the upper part of the front face of the base.

Deeply v-cut letters with minimal serifs and somewhat uneven in both height and width. Small triangular interpuncts throughout. C, O, G consistently large/wide. P is open, R is closed. E has equal bars.
Augustan; normally assumed to honour an original member of the colony, founded in 21 BCE by Augustus
Found at the end of October 1879 on the 'piano della chiesa maggiore e vicino al diruto castello'
Photo J. Prag, courtesy Museo Civico Civico Baldassare Romano, Termini Imerese

A large and well-displayed collection. It begins with three ante-rooms, in the first of which is a rich display of lithics (mainly Neolithic) from the surrounding area. A display of material from Himera (lion-head spouts from the Temple of Victory and red-figure craters being the principle pieces) and a numismatic gallery then follow before the main room which contains Hellenistic and Roman material from Termini Imerese itself. Small finds, sculpture and inscriptions predominate. The museum collection continues upstairs with non-archaeological material (and a single inscription). The courtyard of the building holds a large collection of architectural pieces and inscriptions
Via Marco Tullio Cicerone, 90018 - Termini Imerese (PA)
Inscriptiones Bruttiorum Lucaniae Campaniae Siciliae Sardiniae Latinae. Pars posterior. Inscriptiones Siciliae et Sardiniae

Text encoding: Robert Crellin
CNAEOPOLLIENOCNAEIFILIOTRIBUNOMILITUMLEGIONISXII

Facsimile Images

Apparatus criticus

  • Text from autopsy

English translation

Jonathan Prag

To Gnaeus Pollienus, son of Gnaeus, military tribune of the 12th legion

commentary

This individual is also honoured in ISic000096, found in Termini in 1876. It is suggested also (e.g. Wilson 1990: 42 n.87) that this man is the son of the Pollienus honoured at Haluntium, possibly in the civil war period (ISic001190). A further inscription (ISic000098) records another (anonymous) individual from the same Legio XII Fulminata. The multiple references to this legion encourage the hypothesis that it was veterans of this legion which were settled in the colonial foundation of Augustus in 21 BC (so, e.g., Manganaro 1988: 42). The distinctive breccia from the area of San Marco d'Alunzio suggests a deliberate choice of prestigious material.

Cnaeo Pollieno Cnaei filio tribuno militum legionis XII
Text from autopsy

To Gnaeus Pollienus, son of Gnaeus, military tribune of the 12th legion

This individual is also honoured in ISic000096, found in Termini in 1876. It is suggested also (e.g. Wilson 1990: 42 n.87) that this man is the son of the Pollienus honoured at Haluntium, possibly in the civil war period (ISic001190). A further inscription (ISic000098) records another (anonymous) individual from the same Legio XII Fulminata. The multiple references to this legion encourage the hypothesis that it was veterans of this legion which were settled in the colonial foundation of Augustus in 21 BC (so, e.g., Manganaro 1988: 42). The distinctive breccia from the area of San Marco d'Alunzio suggests a deliberate choice of prestigious material.
